results.possibleIdentity: Richard Lowenberg (born 1946) is an artist, designer, and eco-cultural activist who has been active since the mid-1960s. He studied architecture and design at Pratt Institute and began using photography and film as creative mediums during that time. By 1970, influenced by cybernetics and figures like Buckminster Fuller and Marshall McLuhan, he transitioned into electronic media, including video, audio synthesis, and holography. Lowenberg was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), joining through Robert Whitman. He was part of the founding team of 'The Kitchen' in New York City in 1971 and taught the first Electronic Media Arts class at Pratt Institute. His work frequently explores the 'ecology of the information environment,' utilizing bio-telemetry, remote sensing, and surveillance technologies. Notable projects include using bio-signals from plants and humans to create music and video, such as his 1976 work 'The Secret Life of Plants,' and collaborating with NASA as an artist-in-residence. He has also served as the co-director of Scientists/Artists Research Collaborations (SARC).

segment.text: He studied architecture and design at Pratt Institute and began using photography and film as creative mediums during that time.
By 1970, influenced by cybernetics and figures like Buckminster Fuller and Marshall McLuhan, he transitioned into electronic media, including video, audio synthesis, and holography.
Lowenberg was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), joining through Robert Whitman.
Notable projects include using bio-signals from plants and humans to create music and video, such as his 1976 work 'The Secret Life of Plants,' and collaborating with NASA as an artist-in-residence.
